conclusion_paragraphs: Based on our forensic analysis of 92 documented findings, the property at 4833 W Christine Cir, Glendale, AZ 85308, USA sustained confirmed storm damage from the September 26, 2025 storm event. Chalk-circle-tested hail impacts on the roof surface confirm functional damage to the architectural laminated composition shingles per HAAG Engineering standards, while turbine vent caps on multiple roof slopes exhibit circular soft metal deformation consistent with hail strike signatures from the same event. Active interior water staining across multiple rooms — documented at ceiling planes and wall-ceiling junctions — establishes a direct causal link between the breached roof envelope and ongoing moisture intrusion. Code violations at the chimney base flashing, counter-flashing, and fascia-to-trim junctions further confirm that the storm event compromised critical weather-resistant barriers across the roof system.

The confirmed hail and wind damage to the architectural laminated composition shingle roof system and associated components — including flashing, fascia/trim, soffit junctions, and turbine vents — requires full system replacement rather than spot repair. The existing shingle exposure of 5.2 inches represents a pre-metric product that cannot be matched by any current manufacturer offering, rendering partial replacement technically infeasible under AZ-IRC R905.2.6, which mandates uniform exposure conforming to manufacturer specifications. Additionally, AZ-IRC R905.2.2 requires full roof deck and underlayment inspection beneath the solar panel arrays during replacement, and the failed chimney flashing systems cited under AZ-IRC R903.2, R905.2.8.3, and R1003.9 trigger mandatory remediation once the roof is opened for repair — collectively, these code triggers convert any repair scope into a full replacement scope.

Based on the documented damage, applicable building codes under the AZ-adopted IRC, and industry standards, our analysis identifies the need for full replacement of the roof system including all architectural laminated composition shingles, underlayment, compromised flashing assemblies at the chimney and roof-to-wall transitions, deteriorated fascia/trim boards, and the chimney crown. The scope must also include solar panel removal and reinstallation to permit proper roof deck inspection and code-compliant installation, as well as interior ceiling repairs across the multiple rooms exhibiting confirmed water damage. The systemic nature of the damage — evidenced by active leaks in multiple interior locations and hail impacts distributed across roof slopes — confirms that the roof system failure is building-wide and cannot be addressed through isolated spot repairs.

This forensic determination is grounded in HAAG Engineering residential roof inspection standards for hail and wind damage identification, NRCA guidelines for shingle system replacement thresholds, and the International Residential Code as adopted in Arizona governing weather-resistant barrier integrity, flashing requirements, and chimney construction standards. The chalk-circle impact testing, soft metal deformation analysis, shingle exposure measurement, and interior moisture-mapping methodologies employed in this inspection conform to established forensic protocols recognized across the insurance and roofing industries.

executive_summary: On September 26, 2025, the property at 4833 W Christine Cir, Glendale, AZ 85308, USA sustained damage from a hail-producing storm system. The residence is a two-story dwelling with architectural laminated composition shingle roofing, stucco and brick exterior cladding, and multiple roof-mounted systems including solar panel arrays, package HVAC units, and turbine ventilation. A comprehensive forensic inspection documented 93 photographs across all material systems, including roofing surfaces, soft metals, interior ceilings, exterior trim, and rooftop mechanical equipment.

Hail impact damage was confirmed across multiple material systems through chalk-circle testing and tape-measure verification on the roof surface. Architectural laminated shingles exhibit granule displacement and exposed mat fibers consistent with direct hail strikes, while rooftop soft metals — including turbine vents and HVAC unit casings — display characteristic circular deformation patterns confirming hail of sufficient size and kinetic energy to damage the roof system. Interior inspection of multiple rooms revealed active water staining and saturation on textured ceilings, confirming that the roof membrane has been breached and is permitting moisture intrusion into the building envelope. Exterior fascia and soffit trim at the garage elevation show cracking and separation at the soffit-to-fascia junction, further evidencing storm-related damage to ancillary roof-edge components.

Full roof system replacement is warranted on the basis of non-repairability. The existing shingle exposure measures approximately 5.2 inches, a legacy pre-metric dimension that precludes color, profile, and dimensional matching with any currently manufactured replacement product, rendering spot repair non-compliant with AZ-IRC standards requiring uniform material performance across the roof plane. The presence of extensive solar panel arrays necessitates coordinated removal and reinstallation to access and replace the underlying roof system, and the confirmed interior water intrusion across multiple rooms demonstrates that the compromised roof membrane poses an ongoing risk of structural deterioration if not fully remediated.

photo_integrity.summary: All 19 photos pass integrity review. The photo set documents a two-story residential property (address 4833) with an aging asphalt shingle roof, rooftop solar panel arrays, and interior water damage evidence (ceiling staining in multiple rooms). Photos show consistent lighting, perspectives, and property features across the set. Chalk marks and measurement annotations on roofing materials represent standard inspection methodology. Interior ceiling photos document legitimate water intrusion staining on popcorn-textured ceilings, correlating with the aged roof condition observed in exterior/roof photos. No evidence of staged damage, photo manipulation, digital editing artifacts, or property misrepresentation was detected.

noaa.episode_narrative: Multi-day storm window scan: [2025-09-24] A slow-moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms over multiple days through much of the region. Activity on the 25th was mostly concentrated across higher terrain areas of eastern AZ, fueled by MLCAPE values approaching 2000 J/kg. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, producing rainfall accumulations of over 2 inches in less than one hour across the Miami area leading to significant flash flooding. Multiple streets became rushing currents of water, floodwaters escaped Bloody Tanks Wash in several locations and flooded businesses, there was a lot of muddy and rocky debris, and there were several minor landslides. Severe hail was observed with the thunderstorm activity as well.  The most impactful day of this multi-day episode occurred on the 26th when widespread thunderstorm activity developed across much of south-central AZ as strong upper-level divergence from the low pressure system moved overhead. Fueled by MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg and near 30 kts of 0-6 km shear, scattered thunderstorms developed in the Phoenix area during the mid-morning hours with increasing coverage through the afternoon hours. Initially, most of the storm reports received were large hail, however, as activity became more widespread the hazard quickly turned to flash flooding with numerous reports of water rescues. A fatality due to drowning was reported in Indian Bend Wash in Scottsdale when the individual's vehicle became submerged by significant floodwaters. A few hours after the rainfall dissipated in the Phoenix area, a new area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with very heavy rainfall, moved eastward into southern Gila County, affecting the Globe/Miami area for a second time in just over 24 hours. Gauge reports and doppler radar estimates out of the Globe area measured approximately 2��� of rain, with most of the rain occurring in less than 30 minutes. A stream gauge along Pinal Creek, just south of downtown Globe, reported a 6 foot water rise in 30 minutes. Subsequently, videos and images emerged from downtown Globe depicting catastrophic flooding as the flood wave in Pinal Creek overtopped bridges, inundated businesses, and flooded or swept away numerous vehicles. Around 800 propane tanks were also swept into the floodwaters from a nearby distribution center. Around 66 vehicles were recovered and approximately 288 tons of trash and debris, as well as 8000 cubic yards of dirt, were cleared.  The flooding claimed three lives.  Additional shower and thunderstorm activity materialized on the 27th as the low pressure system was still over southern CA. The more widespread activity was concentrated across portions of southwest AZ during the morning and early afternoon hours with instances of flash flooding reported. Additional activity did develop across south-central AZ as well, including across portions of the Phoenix area, during the afternoon hours. Coverage was more isolated compared to the 26th, but the storms still produced additional flash flooding and severe winds gusts. A thunderstorm cell once again moved through the already flood ravaged area of Globe/Miami in the afternoon. Despite rainfall amounts remaining under an inch with this storm, due to quick storm motion, flash flooding occurred once again in the area, exacerbated by the very saturated soils from the rainfall the previous two days. Following this three-day episode, local, state, and federal disaster declarations were made for the Globe area, a disaster assistance center was opened, and approximately 600 initial and preliminary damage assessments were conducted by AZ Department of Emergency and Military Affairs, finding around 200 private and public properties were destroyed, with the rest experiencing some scale of damage. Not all events are captured in detail in this episode due to the large scale and number of the impacts. ASOS at Phoenix Deer Valley Airport measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph. No damage or injuries were reported.

events.narrative: Powerline down near the area of Claremont Street and North 55th Avenue in Glendale. No injuries reported.
Powerlines down in the block between Thomas Road and McDowell Road and 43rd Avenue and 39th Avenue in Phoenix. No injuries reported.
events.source_detail: NOAA Storm Events — PHOENIX FARM AERO AR

events.narrative: Several very large trees were uprooted in a neighborhood located in the area of 24th street and Thomas in Phoenix. One tree fell onto the roof of a house, damaging the roof, while another tree fell onto a vehicle. Wind gusts were estimated at around 70 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries reported.

Numerous downed power lines in a large area between North 24th to North 40th street and Osborn Road to Red Mountain Freeway in Phoenix. Over 6000 homes were without power. Wind gusts were estimated up to 70 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries reported.

Downed power lines in the area of North 44th Street and Camelhead Road and Camelback Road to Campbell Avenue in Phoenix. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries reported.
events.source_detail: NOAA Storm Events — PAPAGO AAF ARPT

events.narrative: Footage from near the Home Depot and Walmart in the Desert Palms Shopping Center, located at 36th Street and Thomas Road in Phoenix, showed approximately 20-30 downed trees in the parking lot. Wind gusts were estimated upwards of 70 mph. No injuries reported.

The AZ Ice Arcadia Rink located in the area of 38th street and Thomas Road in Phoenix suffered significant damage, with the roof of the building partially peeled off and damage that led to an electrical fire. Dust and debris fell from the damaged roof/ceiling onto the rink and everything else inside. The building's TV satellite dish was also thrown onto the roof of the building next door. Wind gusts, given the severity of the damage, were estimated at around 70 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries reported. Damage estimates were reported at approximately $1 million.
Downed power line reported between Madison and Jackson Streets and between 12th and 14th Streets. No injuries reported.
Reports of power pole down along 19th Street, in between Washington and Jefferson Streets in Phoenix.
Estimated wind gust of 55-60 mph near the corner of Thomas Road and 48th Street in Phoenix. No injuries or damage reported.
events.source_detail: NOAA Storm Events — PAPAGO AAF ARPT

events.narrative: Tree uprooted near the intersection of East University Ave and South College Avenue on the campus of Arizona State University in Tempe. No injuries reported.
Power lines reported down between North Longmore and North Alma School Roads, between Red Mountain Freeway and West University Drive in Mesa. No injuries reported.
Power line reported down from North 80th to North 88th Streets, between East Oak Street and East McDowell Road. No injuries reported.
Power lines down in the area of Chandler Heights Road to Komatke Road, between Hays Road and 189th Avenue in Buckeye. No injuries reported.
Downed power poles reported near West Patterson Road in Buckeye. No injuries reported.

weather.storm_description: Multi-day storm window scan: [2025-09-24] A slow-moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms over multiple days through much of the region. Activity on the 25th was mostly concentrated across higher terrain areas of eastern AZ, fueled by MLCAPE values approaching 2000 J/kg. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, producing rainfall accumulations of over 2 inches in less than one hour across the Miami area leading to significant flash flooding. Multiple streets became rushing currents of water, floodwaters escaped Bloody Tanks Wash in several locations and flooded businesses, there was a lot of muddy and rocky debris, and there were several minor landslides. Severe hail was observed with the thunderstorm activity as well.  The most impactful day of this multi-day episode occurred on the 26th when widespread thunderstorm activity developed across much of south-central AZ as strong upper-level divergence from the low pressure system moved overhead. Fueled by MLCAPE values near 2000 J/kg and near 30 kts of 0-6 km shear, scattered thunderstorms developed in the Phoenix area during the mid-morning hours with increasing coverage through the afternoon hours. Initially, most of the storm reports received were large hail, however, as activity became more widespread the hazard quickly turned to flash flooding with numerous reports of water rescues. A fatality due to drowning was reported in Indian Bend Wash in Scottsdale when the individual's vehicle became submerged by significant floodwaters. A few hours after the rainfall dissipated in the Phoenix area, a new area of strong to severe thunderstorms, with very heavy rainfall, moved eastward into southern Gila County, affecting the Globe/Miami area for a second time in just over 24 hours. Gauge reports and doppler radar estimates out of the Globe area measured approximately 2��� of rain, with most of the rain occurring in less than 30 minutes. A stream gauge along Pinal Creek, just south of downtown Globe, reported a 6 foot water rise in 30 minutes. Subsequently, videos and images emerged from downtown Globe depicting catastrophic flooding as the flood wave in Pinal Creek overtopped bridges, inundated businesses, and flooded or swept away numerous vehicles. Around 800 propane tanks were also swept into the floodwaters from a nearby distribution center. Around 66 vehicles were recovered and approximately 288 tons of trash and debris, as well as 8000 cubic yards of dirt, were cleared.  The flooding claimed three lives.  Additional shower and thunderstorm activity materialized on the 27th as the low pressure system was still over southern CA. The more widespread activity was concentrated across portions of southwest AZ during the morning and early afternoon hours with instances of flash flooding reported. Additional activity did develop across south-central AZ as well, including across portions of the Phoenix area, during the afternoon hours. Coverage was more isolated compared to the 26th, but the storms still produced additional flash flooding and severe winds gusts. A thunderstorm cell once again moved through the already flood ravaged area of Globe/Miami in the afternoon. Despite rainfall amounts remaining under an inch with this storm, due to quick storm motion, flash flooding occurred once again in the area, exacerbated by the very saturated soils from the rainfall the previous two days. Following this three-day episode, local, state, and federal disaster declarations were made for the Globe area, a disaster assistance center was opened, and approximately 600 initial and preliminary damage assessments were conducted by AZ Department of Emergency and Military Affairs, finding around 200 private and public properties were destroyed, with the rest experiencing some scale of damage. Not all events are captured in detail in this episode due to the large scale and number of the impacts.
